title: Keeping Cash on Hand
subtitle: Buffers, forecasting & the liquidity rules

hook.metaphor: Sensible households keep an emergency fund — a few months' expenses in easy-to-reach savings — so a surprise bill or lost income doesn't sink them. Organisations do the same with **liquidity buffers**: a stash of genuinely cash-like assets, held precisely so they can survive a sudden squeeze without a fire sale. Boring, slightly costly, and the difference between a scare and a collapse.
hook.scenario: Two firms face the same sudden cash shock. One kept a buffer of cash and government bonds it could use instantly. The other had 'plenty of assets' — all tied up in property and slow invoices. Same shock, opposite outcomes. What did the first one understand?

learn: The first defence is a **liquidity buffer**: a reserve of high-quality, genuinely liquid assets — cash and things that stay sellable even in stress (regulators call these **HQLA**, High-Quality Liquid Assets, chiefly cash and government bonds). The point is assets that convert to cash *fast and reliably*, especially when everything else is frozen.

Buffers cost something — safe liquid assets earn little — so there's a real tension: hold too little and you're fragile, hold too much and you drag returns. Setting the right buffer is a deliberate trade-off, not an afterthought.

The second defence is **cash-flow forecasting**: projecting money in versus money out over coming days, weeks, and months to spot a squeeze *before* it arrives. Our doomed Level-1 company died partly for lack of this — the tax bill shouldn't have been a surprise.

After 2008, regulators turned buffers into hard rules. The **Basel** framework introduced the **Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR)** — hold enough HQLA to survive a 30-day stress — and the **Net Stable Funding Ratio (NSFR)** — fund long-term assets with stable, longer-term money (directly attacking the maturity-mismatch fragility from Level 3). APRA, RBNZ and RBI apply these. You don't need the formulas; you need the idea: *prove you can survive a defined squeeze.*
Underpinning all of it: never count illiquid assets as part of your buffer. A 'buffer' of property and unpaid invoices is not a buffer — it's exactly the trap the second firm fell into.
bridge: So at work, you can gauge real liquidity strength with three checks: *Is there a genuine buffer of cash-like (HQLA) assets — not property dressed up as cash? Do we forecast cash in vs out to see squeezes coming? And could we survive a defined period of stress (the LCR idea)?* An emergency fund you can actually reach is what turns a cash shock from fatal to merely uncomfortable.

interactions.title: Same shock, opposite outcomes
interactions.prompt: One firm survived a cash shock with a buffer of cash and government bonds; the other had only property and slow invoices. The lesson?

options.label: Both had assets, so both should have been fine.
options.feedback: 'Having assets' isn't the same as having *liquid* assets. Property and unpaid invoices can't pay a bill on Friday. Only genuinely cash-like buffers help in a squeeze.

options.label: A buffer must be genuinely liquid (HQLA) — not illiquid assets dressed up as cash.
options.feedback: Exactly. The survivor held cash and government bonds that convert instantly even in stress. The other counted illiquid assets as a buffer — the classic, fatal self-deception.

options.label: The second firm just had bad luck.
options.feedback: Not luck — a foreseeable failure to hold a real liquidity buffer. The shock was survivable with proper preparation.

interactions.title: Liquidity defences
interactions.prompt: Match each tool to what it does.

pairs.right: A reserve of cash-like assets for a squeeze
Projecting money in vs out to spot squeezes early
Hold enough HQLA to survive ~30 days of stress
Fund long-term assets with stable longer-term money

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Keep an emergency fund of genuinely cash-like assets (HQLA), forecast cash in versus out, and make sure you could survive a defined stress period — and never kid yourself that property or unpaid invoices count as a buffer.”

checkpoint.q: A liquidity buffer should consist of…
options: High-quality liquid assets like cash and government bonds
checkpoint.explain: HQLA convert to cash fast and reliably even in stress — that's what a real buffer needs.

checkpoint.q: The Basel Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR) essentially requires you to…
options: Hold enough liquid assets to survive about a 30-day stress
checkpoint.explain: It's a hard rule that you can survive a defined short, severe liquidity squeeze.

checkpoint.q: Holding a large liquidity buffer involves a trade-off because…
options: Safe liquid assets earn little, so too big a buffer drags returns
Buffers always lose all their value
checkpoint.explain: Too little buffer is fragile; too much hurts returns — sizing it is a deliberate decision.
